title: Ancient Rome
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on ancient Rome!
topic_path: From Roman Republic to Roman Empire

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>"But the Romans have subjected to their rule not portions, but nearly the whole of the world and possess an empire which is not only immeasurably greater than any which preceded it, but need not fear rivalry in the future. In the course of this work it will become more clearly intelligible by what steps this power was acquired.”

From Polybius, _Histories_, Book I, written in the late first century BCE

**The process described by Polybius in this passage is best understood in which of the following contexts?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The expansion of the Roman Republic to control much of the Mediterranean
The expansion of the Roman Republic to control much of Eurasia
The expansion of the Roman Empire to control much of the Mediterranean
The expansion of the Roman Empire to control much of the world

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“To augment the number of persons employed in the administration of the state, he [Augustus] devised several new offices; such as surveyors of the public buildings, of the roads, the aqueducts, and the bed of the Tiber [River]; for the distribution of corn to the people; the prefecture of the city; a triumvirate for the election of the senators; and another for inspecting the several troops of the equestrian order, as often as it was necessary. He revived the office of censor, which had been long disused, and increased the number of praetors.”

From: Suetonius, _The Lives of the Twelve Caesars_

**The actions of Augustus described in this passage contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growth of Roman bureaucracy in the imperial period
The growth of trade due to the expansion of roads
The beginning of census-taking by the Roman government
The decline in importance of Roman political institutions under Augustus

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“The Roman Emperor Gratian committed the task to the Nannienus, a leader of great prudence and skill, joining with him as his colleague with equal power, Mellobaudes, the count-commander of the domestics, as well as the king of the Franks, a man of great courage and renown in war.

>Nannienus took into his consideration the variable chances of fortune, and therefore voted for acting slowly and with caution, while Mellobaudes, hurried away by a fierce desire for fighting, according to his usual custom, was eager at once to march against the enemy and not delay."

From Ammianus, _History_, written in the late fourth century CE.

**The passage above is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The growing use of non-Roman soldiers in the late Roman empire
The distrust that Rome had of non-Romans during the late Roman empire
The final collapse of the Roman empire into smaller kingdoms
The Roman conquest and assimilation of the Frankish kingdom

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The image above is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Ancient road in modern-day Mersin Province in Turkey, circa 100 CE
options.caption: Ancient road in modern-day Mersin Province in Turkey, circa 100 CE. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-images/3803ed0b9fa4db84dacd54042e9e5ec40b44eac7.jpg)

choices.content: Roads fulfilled the Roman Empire's need to project military power
Roads resulted from the construction of trade routes by local communities
Trade brought advances in road building techniques to Rome
Roads allowed the restoration of old Greek and Phoenician military supply routes

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passages below and answer the question. 

>Philosophy is no trick to catch the public; it is not devised for show. It is a matter, not of words, but of facts . . . It moulds and constructs the soul; it orders our life, guides our conduct, shows us what we should do and what we should leave undone; it sits at the helm and directs our course as we waver amid uncertainties. Without it, no one can live fearlessly or in peace of mind.

From: Seneca, _Moral Letters to Lucilius, XVI._ (Seneca was a Roman philosopher and playwright who lived from 4 BCE to 65 CE.)

>All men by nature desire to know. An indication of this is the delight we take in our senses; for even apart from their usefulness they are loved for themselves; and above all others the sense of sight. For not only with a view to action, but even when we are not going to do anything, we prefer seeing (one might say) to everything else. The reason is that [sight], most of all the senses, makes us know and brings to light many differences between things.

From: Aristotle, _Metaphysics_. (Aristotle was a Greek philosopher who lived from 384 to 322 BCE.)


**Taken together, these passages could _best_ be used as evidence for which of the following claims?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Some Greek and Roman philosophies emphasized logic, observation, and  investigation
Some Greek and Roman philosophies emphasized that people's lives were guided by forces they could not understand
Some Greek and Roman philosophies emphasized that seeing was the only way to obtain knowledge.
Some Greek and Roman philosophies emphasized that seeking knowledge was not of primary importance.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>We should believe the Sun to be life itself and, to speak more plainly, the soul of the whole world. Indeed, the Sun is the principal governance of nature, and no less than a God or divine power, considering his works and operations. 

>The Sun gives light to all things, and rids them of darkness; 

>He hides the other stars, and shows them again; 

>He orders the seasons in their alternative course

>He tempers the year, arising ever fresh and new again, for the benefit and good of the world. 

From: Pliny the Elder, _Natural History_. Pliny was a Roman philosopher who lived from 23 to 79 CE. 

**The ideas expressed in the passage above could best be used as evidence for which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The influence of animistic beliefs in the Roman Empire
The spread of Christian iconography in the Roman Empire
The establishment of monotheism in the Roman Empire
The importance of divine agriculture to the Roman Empire

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“[Chinese sources] describe a remarkable increase in the incidence of epidemics during the reign of Emperor Ling, with outbreaks recorded in 171, 173, 179, 182, and 185 [CE] . . . At the other end of the Eurasian landmass the Antonine plague . . . afflicted the Roman empire for fifteen years from the mid-160’s [CE] . . . It is said to have been brought [to Rome] by troops returning from campaign in Mesopotamia, though it could, of course, have come from further East.” 

From Rafe de Crespigny, _Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to the Three Kingdoms_, 2007. 

**The author’s comment that the plague _could_ have come from farther east is best understood in the context of which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Silk Road trade was occurring at this time  between China and Mesopotamia
Roman military campaigns went farther east than Mesopotamia
A direct conflict was occurring between the Roman Empire and Han China
Christian missionaries were going from the Roman Empire to Han China

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>“[Chinese sources] describe a remarkable increase in the incidence of epidemics during the reign of Emperor Ling, with outbreaks recorded in 171, 173, 179, 182, and 185 [CE] . . . At the other end of the Eurasian landmass the Antonine plague . . . afflicted the Roman empire for fifteen years from the mid-160’s [CE] . . . It is said to have been brought [to Rome] by troops returning from campaign in Mesopotamia, though it could, of course, have come from further East.” 

From Rafe de Crespigny, _Biographical Dictionary of Later Han to the Three Kingdoms_, 2007. 

**All of the following are factually accurate. What similarity between the Roman Empire and Han China made them _most vulnerable_ to outbreaks of contagious disease?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both were urbanized and engaged in extensive trade
Both ruled over large and diverse populations
Both suffered from internal political conflict and revolts
Both built up large and powerful militaries

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question. 

>"Caesar Augustus . . . proclaims: Since the nation of the Jews and Hyrcanus, their high priest, have been found grateful to the people of the Romans, not only in the present but also in the past . . . it seems good to me and to my advisory council . . . that the Jews shall use their own customs in accordance with their ancestral law, just as they used to use them in the time of Hyrcanus, the high priest of their highest god; and that their sacred offerings shall be inviolable and shall be sent to Jerusalem and shall be paid to the financial officials of Jerusalem"

From the Edict of Augustus on Jewish Rights, 1 BCE

**Which of the following best explains why the agreement made in this passage was made?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The conquest of Jerusalem by Roman forces led to Rome imposing strict control over Jerusalem
The development of Christianity in the Roman Empire forced Rome to be more religiously tolerant
The Jewish-Roman wars forced Rome to offer Jews more religious freedom in order to prevent violence
Rome offered legal protections for Jewish religious and cultural practices to maintain good relations

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 3]]


**The images shown above best represent which of the following similarities between classical empires?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Section of Hadrian’s Wall in the northern part of modern-day England.
options.caption: Section of ruins from Hadrian’s Wall in the northern part of modern-day England. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-images/1b59c7595aa0f1f92a60bc6ef61bd8743dbdd590.jpg)

options.alt: Ruins of the Great Wall from the Han dynasty, near Dunhuang, China.
options.caption: Ruins of the Great Wall from the Han dynasty, near Dunhuang, China. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hanmuren.JPG)

choices.content: The need to secure frontier regions
The spread of urban planning to frontier regions
The need to control enslaved populations
The use of roads to facilitate trade
